rank_note: Rank intentionally null. site/src/data/indexes/us-states.json contains only 21 of 51 jurisdictions; ranks 9-38 were lost in the original legacy-HTML extraction and the survivors were renumbered contiguously, so the displayed rank column is not national rank. Oregon must not be assigned a rank until the index is rebuilt with all 51 jurisdictions.
calibration_note: Not calibrated to published us-states rank order (known corrupt). Calibrated to methodology anchors and to the states assessed at full depth on 2026-07-19: Georgia 26.9, Iowa 30.6, Arizona 36.2, Ohio 38.1, Alaska 47.5, Colorado 52.5, Michigan 53.8, Delaware 53.8, Pennsylvania 54.4, New York 56.9, Illinois 60.0. Oregon at 50.6 sits between Alaska and Colorado. Composite verified by running computeCompositeFromDimensions from site/scripts/lib/scoring.mjs on the sidecar rollups {AWR:3.6, EMP:2.8, ACT:2.6, EQU:3.4, BND:2.4, ACC:3, SYS:3.6, INT:2.8}; raw output: {"composite":50.6,"band":"Functional","integrationPremium":0}. Supporting arithmetic: dimension mean 3.0250, base composite 50.625, stdDev 0.4294 (consistencyMult 1.0), 8 of 8 dimensions below 4.0 so weaknessFactor 0 and integrationPremium 0. Sensitivity case, also run through computeCompositeFromDimensions rather than computed by hand: raising ACT to 3.0 and BND to 2.8 gives raw output {"composite":53.1,"band":"Functional","integrationPremium":0}, still Functional and still zero premium, so the band does not turn on the two weakest dimensions.
attribution_note: Scores Oregon state conduct only. Measure 110 (2020) and its successor HB 4002 (2024) are fully scored as state conduct: the Legislature amended the framework in SB 755 (2021), HB 2513 (2023), HB 4002 (2024) and SB 610 (2025), and the Oregon Health Authority administered the roughly $800 million in spending. Delivery of HB 4002 deflection is devolved to the 36 counties and is elective at law-enforcement discretion. County-level program quality is NOT scored against the state. What is scored against the state is the design choice: Oregon built an opt-in structure, 28 of 36 counties sought state deflection funding, and residents of the remaining 8 counties have no deflection pathway at all. That gap is a consequence of state design, and it is scored under EQ1 Universality, EQ4 Access Design and B4 Refusal Ethics. The Medicaid 1115 demonstration required federal CMS approval, but Oregon designed and requested the first-in-nation housing, nutrition and climate benefit, so the initiative is credited to Oregon under S1, S4 and EQ2 rather than to the federal approver. Lane v. Brown and the Mink/Bowman competency orders are federal court proceedings; Oregon is scored for its own compliance conduct (full completion in Lane, continuing noncompliance and accruing fines in Mink), not for the existence of the orders. The 2020 Labor Day wildfire damages litigation runs against PacifiCorp, a private utility, and is not scored as state harm; only the Oregon Public Utility Commission decision declining to cap that utility liability is scored, under S4.
key_evidence: Oregon Secretary of State audit 2025-29, published 2025-12-17, found the state spent roughly $800 million on Measure 110 programs but the Oregon Health Authority cannot say how many people received services or whether those programs helped. Auditors issued six recommendations; the Oregon Health Authority agreed with three.

Oregon is the first state in the nation to use Medicaid to pay for housing, nutrition and climate needs. Covered items include air conditioners, heaters, air filtration devices and back-up generators, under the 2022-2027 1115 demonstration waiver.

More than 1,000 criminal cases were being dismissed as of 2026-03-06 because Oregon could not provide court-appointed lawyers. Unrepresented defendants stood at about 1,200, down from a peak of 3,500 in May 2025.

Oregon recorded its highest-ever rate of children seriously harmed in foster care in federal fiscal year 2025, at 24.7 incidents per 100,000 care days against a federal target of 9.07. The federal Child and Family Services Review found Oregon not in substantial conformity with any outcome.

Oregon is accruing court fines above $2 million on a per-person per-day basis for failing the Mink order. The average wait for jail-held people needing competency restoration at the Oregon State Hospital was 21 days against a court-mandated maximum of 7 days.

Oregon closed every sheltered workshop in the state and placed 1,138 former workshop employees into competitive jobs ahead of deadline, completing the Lane v. Brown agreement that the U.S. Department of Justice concluded on 2022-07-25.

evidence.claim: roughly $800M spent with no usable outcome measurement, driving AC3 Efficacy and A1 data infrastructure down

evidence.quote: Oregon Health Authority: Measure 110 Lacks Stability, Coordination, and Clear Results
evidence.claim: independently elected auditor published an unflattering finding with a public agency response, supporting AB3 Transparency and A3 Blind Spot Mitigation

evidence.quote: the first in the nation to introduce Medicaid funding for interventions that address social needs related to housing, nutrition, and climate change to improve health outcomes and lower program costs
evidence.claim: genuine upstream structural investment, the strongest single item in the assessment, supporting S1, S4 and EQ2

evidence.quote: More than 1,000 criminal cases in Oregon are in the process of being dismissed because the state could not provide defendants with court-appointed attorneys
evidence.claim: core constitutional service failure driving AC1 Responsiveness and B4 Refusal Ethics to 2

evidence.quote: Oregon was not in substantial conformity with any of the outcomes

evidence.quote: The state is accruing fines now in excess of $2 million on a per person per day basis for noncompliance with the court order
evidence.claim: Oregon absorbs court penalties rather than meeting the commitment, driving I1 Consistency Under Pressure to 2

evidence.quote: By 2022, the state completely shut down all sheltered workshops and helped 1,138 former workshop employees find other jobs, ahead of last month’s deadline
evidence.claim: completed multi-year reparative and autonomy-building commitment, the basis for B2 at 4 and EQ5 at 4

evidence.quote: retaliated against health care staff when they raised reasonable concerns about patient care across Oregon’s 12 prisons
evidence.claim: internal culture punished people who surfaced harm, driving I3 Internal Consistency and E4 Validation to 2

evidence.quote: Oregon’s Housing Authority disagrees with audit’s finding of $426M in untraced spending

evidence.quote: nearly 9,000 vacancies across state government, and high vacancies among frontline workers represented by SEIU means poorer service for Oregonians
evidence.claim: compassionate work is not resourced from a stable base, driving B1 Self-Sustainability to 2

evidence.quote: Deflection programs are now in the early stages in 28 of Oregon’s 36 counties
evidence.claim: 8 counties left with no deflection pathway, a state design choice scored under EQ1, EQ4 and B4

evidence.quote: requires state agencies to create community engagement plans and Racial Equity Impact Statements

evidence.source: Office of the Governor of Oregon, Racial Justice Council
